sections.about: This metric measures water consumed, typically withdrawal minus discharge, and is disclosed under BRSR Principle 6 Essential Indicator water consumption. It is a stronger stress indicator than withdrawal where discharge is material. A lower value indicates better performance.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: Jindal is consistently best, moving from 11.68 million kL in FY2023 to 14.41 million kL in FY2025. JSW is second despite increasing to 53.25 million kL. SAIL worsens materially to 87.77 million kL in FY2025. Tata remains highest at 98.61 million kL in FY2025, despite its water strategy focused on recycling, treated municipal wastewater and ZED. The gap indicates that Tata’s operating scale and integrated-route water losses through cooling, evaporation, slag handling, gas cleaning and captive utilities remain substantial.
sections.tataPositioning: In FY2025, Tata ranks 4th. Jindal is best at 14.41 million kL, and Tata is approximately 584% higher. Tata’s rank is unchanged at 4th across all three years; the FY2024-25 increase from 88.35 million kL to 98.61 million kL is operationally adverse.
sections.targets: Tata states it aims to minimise water usage and maximise recycling, including reducing river freshwater withdrawal and recovering water through CETP, STPs and storm run-off streams. SAIL states an ZLD goal and recirculation of approximately 128 million m³/year of wastewater across ISPs in FY2024-25.
sections.comparabilityNotes: Absolute consumption is scale-sensitive. Physical-output intensity is more useful for operational benchmarking. Tata Steel FY2022-23 total water consumption was not explicitly reported on a purely standalone basis; it was reported collectively for Tata Steel India and its Indian subsidiaries.
